subject: dumpster divers
tagline: World of Warcraft for dumpster divers
one_liner: A persistent-world loot MMO where the raids are municipal and the loot is whatever the fourth floor left at the curb.
brand_voice_notes: Deadpan endgame-guild energy applied to trash collection. Every discarded object gets a WoW rarity tier. Bulk-trash night is a raid. Property managers are world bosses, guilds are dive parties, and the appraisal engine speaks in item stats. Faux-corporate, faintly patch-notes, never winking. The bit: the company genuinely believes municipal waste is content and has priced companionship and dignity by the seat.

hero.sub: Haul runs your block as a persistent-world loot game. Real curbs, live raids, and an appraisal engine that grades a warped Billy shelf as Rare and a mattress as Bind-on-Pickup. Queue up, pull the bin, roll need or greed with forty strangers on your street.

features.title: Loot Appraisal Engine
features.blurb: A vision model grades every object at the curb on the standard rarity scale. A cracked rice cooker pings Uncommon. An untouched Eames knockoff pings Epic. A mattress is Bind-on-Pickup and cannot be traded, mailed, or explained to your partner.

features.title: 40-Diver Raid Scheduling
features.blurb: Bulk-trash night is endgame content. Assemble a raid, assign roles, and pull on a shared timer. The tank holds the lid. Healers handle tetanus risk. If someone ninjas the credenza, that is a guild matter, not a Haul matter.

features.title: Live Threat Table
features.blurb: A real-time map of contested bins. When a property manager pulls aggro, or a rival guild flags your alley, everyone in range gets pinged and a wipe timer starts. You have roughly ninety seconds before the porch light comes on.

pricing_tiers.name: Free Trial · Levels 1 to 19
pricing_tiers.tagline: Standard curb access. Solo only. Loot capped at Uncommon.
perks: One trash bag of bank space

pricing_tiers.name: Season Pass
pricing_tiers.tagline: The main-character tier. Raids, guild, and the full rarity spectrum.
perks: Transmog: make the shelf photograph like new

pricing_tiers.name: Founding Guildmaster
pricing_tiers.tagline: You own a spawn point. We do not recommend telling people this.
perks: Claim a city block as guild territory
Set the loot rules for everyone on it

testimonials.quote: Pulled a mid-century credenza off a fourth-floor drop before the property manager finished his coffee. My guild cut me for ninjaing it. Worth every point of reputation.
testimonials.name: Dale Prewitt
testimonials.title: Raid Lead, Thursday-Night Salvage Cohort, Battle Creek

testimonials.quote: I main furniture. Been Best-in-Slot on load-bearing since Season 2. The appraisal engine is the first system that has ever actually seen my build.
testimonials.name: Marisol Tang
testimonials.title: BiS Furniture Main, 3,200 Hauls Logged

testimonials.quote: Wipe timer saved the whole party. Manager pulled and we were already three alleys out with the loot mailed. That is what forty dollars a year is for.
testimonials.name: Gunnar Aoki
testimonials.title: Off-Tank on Lid Duty, Precinct 7 Dive Collective

demo_screen_concept: A loot-council and inventory console. A grid of tonight's scavenged curbside objects rendered as rarity-bordered inventory slots (gray common, green uncommon, blue rare, purple epic, orange legendary), each with a hover tooltip written like a game item: 'Warped Billy Shelf, Rare, +3 Curb Appeal, +12 Storage, Binds when picked up.' A side quest log names the active raid ('4th and Alder bulk drop, 40 divers queued'), an XP bar tracks the diver's level, and each item carries a Roll Need / Greed action that fakes a d100 and assigns the loot to a raid member. Interactive: rarity filter chips, tab between Inventory and Raid Roster, item tooltips, and a need-or-greed roll modal that resolves live.

images.rationale: Typographic engraved fantasy game-UI hero, skipped (the wordmark plate and filigree carry it, no photo earns a slot). Demo is an icon/rarity-slot loot console, skipped (item icons, not photo-shaped). Budget spent on the always-applicable fallback: three deadpan corporate headshots of the named testimonial divers, shot as dim guild-hall portraits to match the dark charcoal + antique-gold palette.

items.prompt: Editorial corporate headshot of a weathered middle-aged working-class man in a worn canvas work jacket, dim guild-hall lighting, antique-gold rim light (#C9A227), charcoal near-black background (#16130F). No text/logos/UI.

Editorial corporate headshot of a practical woman in her late thirties, plaid flannel over a plain tee, dim guild-hall lighting, antique-gold rim light (#C9A227), charcoal near-black background (#16130F). No text/logos/UI.

Editorial corporate headshot of a burly young man in a knit beanie and heavy work hoodie with gloves at the collar, dim guild-hall lighting, antique-gold rim light (#C9A227), charcoal near-black background (#16130F). No text/logos/UI.

design_direction.style: Fantasy RPG questlog / illuminated MMO game-UI. The page is staged as the client for a heavily funded games company that has decided municipal waste is a persistent-world loot economy and prices dignity by the seat. A dark tavern-stone ground (deep warm charcoal) carries faint engraved grid-etching and thin antique-gold filigree corners on every panel, so each section reads as a carved character-sheet plate rather than a webpage. Panels are flat stone plates with 1px antique-gold hairline borders, small 6px radii, engraved corner flourishes, and a subtle inner bevel; no glass, no blur veils, no soft drop shadows. One antique gold carries the wordmark, headlines, filigree, hairlines, and every structural label. The WoW item-rarity ladder (gray common, green uncommon, blue rare, purple epic, orange legendary) is the ONLY chromatic system on the page and is used strictly to grade loot in the demo, rarity chips, and the pricing ladder; one of those, epic purple, is the rationed CTA/loot accent reserved for the single primary CTA per section, the live raid-status rune, and Epic-or-better loot glows, and appears almost nowhere else as flat fill. Cinzel sets the wordmark and headlines in inscriptional Roman fantasy caps with the gravity of a game-title logo; Spectral carries calm, faintly lore-manual body copy; a mono (Spline Sans Mono) sets every figure that pretends to be game telemetry (rarity scores, roll results, seat counts, tier prices, wipe timers, XP) with tabular figures in engraved label wells, so it reads like an item stat block, not marketing. Motion is game-UI restrained: loot tooltips fade in on hover, Epic-or-better slots pulse a soft rune-glow on a 3s cycle, the XP bar fills once on scroll, plates rise 6px and fade in on scroll, the CTA depresses 1px and its rune brightens on press, the demo d100 roll ticks up once and settles, all gated behind prefers-reduced-motion. No light mode, no neon synthwave, no CRT scanlines, no glass, no clay, no bento lattice, no marquee, no pixel-art, no emoji. Reads like a games company that files a stranger's discarded couch under 'world drop' and lists the porch light coming on as a mechanic.
design_direction.archetype: Gaming / entertainment MMO landing page with a scroll-through pitch and one interactive loot console demo.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Item tooltips fade in on hover; Epic+ loot slots pulse a soft rune-glow (3s); XP bar fills once on scroll; plates rise 6px + fade on scroll; CTA rune brightens and depresses 1px on press; demo d100 ticks and settles. All gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Left-weighted hero: Cinzel wordmark 'Haul' with a small antique-gold filigree bracket, a mono kicker rune (RAID TIER: MUNICIPAL), the h1 in large Cinzel caps, Spectral sub, and the epic-purple 'Create your diver' CTA beside a ghost 'Open the loot console' link. A right-side character-sheet plate previews a single rarity-bordered loot slot (the Warped Billy Shelf) with a live stat tooltip and an XP bar, framed in engraved gold filigree. Import Cinzel, Spectral, and Spline Sans Mono. Rarity colors used only to grade loot, never as decoration.
